tweets: Buying a home is exciting but common mistakes cost thousands. Let us walk through five pitfalls to avoid.
Mistake 1: Not checking your credit score before applying. A 20-point difference can raise your rate by 0.5%.
Mistake 2: Skipping pre-approval. Sellers take you seriously only with a pre-approval letter in hand.
Mistake 3: Ignoring hidden costs like property taxes, insurance, and maintenance. These add 1-2% of home value annually.
Mistake 4: Maxing out your budget. Lenders approve you for more than you should spend. Stick to 28% of gross income.
New to investing? Start here. Five simple steps to build your first portfolio without stress.
Step 1: Set an emergency fund first. Cover 3-6 months of expenses before investing a dime.
Step 2: Choose low-cost index funds or ETFs. They diversify your money and keep fees under 0.10%.
Step 3: Use dollar-cost averaging. Invest a fixed amount monthly, regardless of market ups and downs.
Step 4: Reinvest dividends. Let your earnings compound automatically for faster growth.
Debt feels overwhelming, but a clear method makes it manageable. Compare two popular payoff strategies.
Snowball method: List debts from smallest to largest balance. Pay minimums on all except the smallest.
Put every extra dollar toward the smallest debt. Once paid, roll that payment to the next smallest.
Avalanche method: List debts by interest rate from highest to lowest. Pay minimums on all except the highest APR.
Avalanche saves more interest, but snowball gives psychological wins. Choose what keeps you motivated.
Retirement planning does not have to be complex. Follow these five tips to secure your future.
Tip 1: Start now. Time is your biggest ally. Even small amounts grow massively over decades.
Tip 2: Max out employer 401(k) match first. That is an instant 50-100% return on your money.
Tip 3: Contribute to a Roth IRA if eligible. Tax-free growth is powerful for long-term gains.
Tip 4: Increase savings by 1% each year. You will barely notice, but your balance will thank you.
